What’s All This Then?

When I was a kid in the 80s, I loved a puzzle book. Not crosswords so much, as my general knowledge was rubbish, but word searches, spot the difference, criss cross, pretty much any kind of number or word puzzle... I loved it. But my absolute favourite was the Logic Problem (or Logigram).

pic of the Logic Problems books

Despite the apparently vague nature of the title, every puzzle was basically the same. You were supplied with a grid, a tortured setup, and an incomplete list of facts, from which your goal was to extraopolate all the missing information.

A typical puzzle would include 5 entities, each with 4 distinct properties, each of which uniquely matches 1 out of five possible values. For example, your five entities might be a person and you might have to identify each person's name, job, age, and favourite colour. Importantly, no two entities shared any properties, so for example, if Stephen is a dentist, none of the others are a dentist, and so on.

pic of a logic problem grid

I'm not going to include an entire how-to here, but basically the clues provided give you just enough information to fill in the grid, by cross-referencing what you know in different categories.

OK, but why now?

In 2023, in time for Christmas, bookshops across the land welcomed G.T. Karber's "Murdle" onto their shelves, triggering a Logic Problem Revival (at least in my house).
